---
title: "control"
format: html
---

# CONTROL STRUCTURES AND ERROR CHECKING

## WHAT WE’VE LEARNED

- how to write a function
- how to generate data
- running functions with data

## FLOW CONTROL

Another KEY concept is flow control

Allowing your function to “do” different things depending on a conditions

CLASSIC example is *IF* then *ELSE*

## IF ELSE IN R

Two ways in R

- *ifelse* function

  - ifelse(condition, TRUE, FALSE)

  - -best used for simple application

- if..else structure

  - more formal way of deciding on two paths

## Example

```{r}
a = 20
classr  = ifelse(a > 90, "high","low")
classr
```

```{r}
# also works with multiple values
a = runif(min=1, max=100, n=5)
a
```

```{r}
# ifelse can work with vectors
classr  = ifelse(a > 90, "high","low")
classr

```

```{r}
classr = ifelse((a > 90) & (a/2 > 10), "high", "low")
# and = &

classr = ifelse((a > 90) | (a/2 < 10), "high", "low")
# or = |

a = runif(min=1, max=100, n=5)
classr = ifelse((a > 90) | (a < 10), "good", "bad")

classr = ifelse(!(a > 90), "good", "bad")
# not = !
```

## FLOW CONTROL IN FUNCTIONS

best to use if {} else {}

see example in *summarize_diversity2.R*

```{r}
library(here)

source(here("Week6/summarize_diversity2.R"))

flowers = sample(c("rose","peony","daisy"), replace=T, prob = c(0.1, 0.8, 0.1),size=30)

summarize_diversity(flowers, index="B")$simpson

summarize_diversity(flowers, index= "A")$simpson
```

## MULTIPLE CONDITIONS

If you have multiple conditions we can use *case_when*, requires *tidyverse* library - *switch* is another option

```{r}
a = runif(min=1, max=60, n=5)
classr = ifelse((a > 10), ifelse(a > 50, "high", "med"), "low")
```

## FISHING EXAMPLE

Here’s a simple example of how it works

Imagine you are recording fish catches at different locations (we are going to simulate this by sampling )

Fish have grades…

grade A, B, C

- steelhead are A,
- tuna are B
- everything else is C

R and other languages have ways to do this multiple alternatives flow control in R an example is

*case_when*

case_when(

condition \~ response,

condition \~ response … )

First a simple example where we only have one fist

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
fish = "steelhead"
case_when((fish =="steelhead")  ~ "A", (fish =="tuna") ~ "B", !(fish == "tuna") & !(fish=="steelhead") ~ "C")
```

## MAKE AN OCEAN

- notice how we build a function to make this 'cleaner'

```{r}
# apply to all of our ocean
# start by making a function - this will be cleaner!
classify_fish = function(fish) {
 class=  case_when((fish =="steelhead")  ~ "A", (fish =="tuna") ~ "B", !(fish == "tuna") & !(fish=="steelhead") ~ "C")
 return(class) }

classify_fish("steelhead")

classify_fish("cod")
```

```{r}
# apply to our ocean
possible.fish = c("salmon","steelhead","shark","tuna","cod")
catch2 = base::sample(possible.fish, size=20, replace=T)
catch2

classify_fish(catch2)
```

## A MORE INTERESTING EXAMPLE

Lets imagine that we are monitoring pollution in a lake, and we want to write a function that will let us know (flag) if risk associated with nutrient pollution are high, medium or low

From ecological studies, we know that

*High Risk Conditions*

- risk is high if water temperature is greater than a threshold for more than 5 days, and
- mean nutrient concentration is greater than a high threshold

*Medium Risk Conditions*

- Risk is medium if water temperature is greater than a threshold for more than 5 days and
- mean nutrient concentration is greater than a medium threshold

*Everything else is low risk*

## DESIGN THE FUNCTION

**Inputs:**

- nutrient concentration for at least 5 days
- temperature for at least 5 days
- thresholds for temperature and nutrient (with default values)

**Output:**

- Mean Nutrient Concentration
- Pollution Risk as “low”, “med” or “high”

Take a look at classify_lake

- example of while
- example of flow control with if..else and case_when

```{r}
source(here("Week6/classify_lake.R"))

# generate some data to try the function
temperature = runif(min=4, max=35, n=30)
nutrient = runif(min=5, max=40, n=30)

# try it
res=classify_lake(temperature=temperature, nutrient=nutrient)

# lets pick some numbers we know should give us a low value
nutrient = runif(min=0, max=5, n=30)
classify_lake(temperature=temperature, nutrient=nutrient)
```

```{r}
# now try high
# use repeat to generate the same value multiple times
nutrient = rep(50, times=30)
temperature = rep(25, times=30)
classify_lake(temperature=temperature, nutrient=nutrient)
```

## REVIEW.

- We are using functions as a way to design modular programs (that might involve many functions that are linked together)
- We always start with clearly defininng our goal for the whole program and each piece (and diagramming this if needed for programs made of multiple functions)
- Always write your function in a text editor and then copy into R
- By convention we name files with functions in them by the name of the function.R e.g. **power_gen_orig.R**
- you can have R read a text file by source(“power_gen.R”) - make sure you are in the right working directory
- keep organized by keeping all functions in a subdirectory called R
- Eventually we will want our function to be part of a package (a library of many functions) - to create a package you must use this convention (name.R) place all function in a directory called “R”

## ERROR CHECKING

What will your function do if user gives you garbage data, imagine that you have a function that computes microbial growth rates with temperature

- Two options

1.  error-checking (nice) if temperature \< -100 or \> 100

- set results to NA
- or exit function with a warming

2.  assume user reads the contract :) return unrealistic values

    if input -999.0, will still try to output growth rate Error-checking is helpful if you are going to build a model made up of many functions- why?

```{r}
data = c("a", "b")
mean(data)
```


## HOW TO ADD ERROR CHECKING

Consider this example (I’m going to put the function definition inline for now so you can easily see it - later we will save to our R subdirectory for reuse)

```{r}
power_gen = function(height, flow, rho=1000, g=9.8, Keff=0.8) {
  # make sure inputs are positive and if not set as NA so result will be NA
   if (height < 0) height=NA
  
 # an alterative is to exit the function prematurely 
  if (flow < 0) return("Flow cannot be less than 0")
  #    return("Height cannot be less than 0")
      
  # calculate power
    result = rho * height * flow * g * Keff
  return(result)
}

power_gen(2,3)

power_gen(-2,3)

power_gen(2, -3)
```
